skill.name: pylabrobot
skill.description: PyLabRobot guidance for designing, simulating, and reviewing laboratory automation protocols across liquid handlers, plate readers, pumps, heater shakers, incubators, centrifuges, and deck resources.
skill.summary: Design and review PyLabRobot laboratory automation workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and environment patterns, but most command findings are Markdown code examples or inline backticks. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. Risk remains medium because the skill intentionally helps users generate code for real laboratory hardware, local network device APIs, file state persistence, and environment-controlled hardware mode.

medium_findings.title: Legitimate Real-World Laboratory Hardware Control
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk: the skill documents code that initializes and commands liquid handlers, plate readers, heater shakers, and related lab devices. This is expected for the skill, but generated protocols can move liquids, open trays, or set temperatures when users run them against real hardware.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes and demonstrates physical device setup and control. This is legitimate functionality, but it has real-world operational impact if copied into a live lab environment.

medium_findings.title: Local Network Hardware Connection Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with legitimate context: the skill includes Opentrons backend examples that connect to a local robot IP address and references HTTP API communication. The examples are not exfiltration, but users must replace placeholders and restrict access to trusted lab networks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network target is an example private IP address for a lab robot, not an external collection endpoint. The risk is medium because model-generated code could connect to the wrong local device if reused without review.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the many Ruby or shell backtick findings arise from Markdown inline code and fenced Python examples. No executable shell helper, subprocess invocation, or malicious command runner was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are documentation code blocks or inline Markdown formatting. The surrounding context teaches PyLabRobot usage rather than executing shell commands from the skill.

low_findings.title: JSON State File Writes Are Local Workflow Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for filesystem access with low severity: examples write and read local JSON state files for deck or plate state. This is normal protocol reproducibility behavior and does not show secret collection or arbitrary file targeting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use fixed local JSON filenames and serialize lab resource state. There is no evidence that sensitive files are read or transmitted.

low_findings.title: Environment Flag Can Switch From Simulation To Hardware
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with low severity: an example uses USE_HARDWARE from the environment to choose a real hardware backend. This is useful for deployment, but production workflows should require explicit operator approval before enabling hardware.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is directly shown and changes backend behavior. The risk is limited because it is an example and does not expose credentials or network data.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: hardcoded URLs point to PyLabRobot documentation, GitHub, community, and PyPI resources. No suspicious external endpoint or data transfer behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are public documentation and package references for the named project. They are not used in executable network requests within the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary domain words and identifiers such as HeaterShaker, ThermoShake, or deck/resource names. No hashing, encryption, password storage, or cryptographic decision logic was found in the sampled locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain lab equipment names or resource setup, not cryptographic APIs. No evidence of MD5, SHA usage for security, or weak cipher selection was found in these sampled findings.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Script Findings Are Python Import Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: dynamic import alerts point to normal Python import statements inside documentation examples. They do not load user-controlled modules or execute downloaded scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples import named PyLabRobot resource classes from static package paths. There is no variable module name, remote code loading, or plugin execution mechanism.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Setup Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: system reconnaissance alerts map to platform support notes, communication requirements, and setup guidance for lab hardware. No host fingerprinting, port scanning, or inventory collection behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text describes supported operating systems, device communication, and visual inspection features. It does not instruct the model to enumerate systems or discover unauthorized network targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Physical Device Operations In Generated Protocols
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches operations that can affect physical lab devices when executed, including liquid transfer, plate reader tray movement, and temperature control. Require simulation and human review before hardware execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The operations are explicit in the examples and are central to the skill. The concern is operational safety rather than malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Private IP Hardware Endpoint Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use a private IP address for Opentrons robot communication. This should be treated as a placeholder and verified against the intended instrument and network segment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The private IP address appears in multiple backend examples and is labeled as the robot IP. It is not malicious but can be hazardous if copied without modification.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Controlled Hardware Mode
dangerous_patterns.description: An example switches to a real hardware backend when USE_HARDWARE is true. Add explicit confirmation gates in production protocols.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is shown directly in the example. The risk depends on how users deploy the generated workflow.

content.value_statement: Lab automation protocols are difficult to adapt across instruments and deck layouts. This skill helps create PyLabRobot workflows for simulation, hardware setup, liquid handling, and analytical devices.
actual_capabilities: Explains PyLabRobot setup for liquid handlers, plate readers, pumps, heater shakers, incubators, and centrifuges.
Provides examples for deck layouts, resources, plates, tip racks, carriers, troughs, and state serialization.
Guides protocol design for aspirate, dispense, transfer, serial dilution, plate replication, and tip tracking workflows.
Shows how to switch between simulation backends and supported hardware backends such as Hamilton STAR and Opentrons OT-2.
Supports visualization and simulation workflows for checking deck state before physical execution.
limitations: It does not verify that a generated protocol is safe for a specific instrument or laboratory process.
It does not replace vendor manuals, calibration procedures, or institutional safety review.
It cannot confirm live device connectivity, firmware compatibility, or deck geometry without the user environment.
It may include example IP addresses and file names that must be changed before use.

use_cases.title: Prototype liquid handling protocols
use_cases.description: Create a first PyLabRobot protocol for transfers, serial dilutions, plate replication, and tip handling.

use_cases.title: Review deck and resource models
use_cases.description: Check whether plates, tips, carriers, and saved states are represented consistently before a lab run.

use_cases.title: Plan instrument integration
use_cases.description: Map workflows that combine liquid handlers with plate readers, scales, heater shakers, centrifuges, or pumps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simulated transfer protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PyLabRobot protocol that simulates transferring 100 microliters from one 96-well plate to another. Include deck resources, tips, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a protocol to my robot
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt this PyLabRobot workflow for my hardware backend and deck layout. Highlight every placeholder I must verify before running on real hardware.

prompt_templates.title: Add tracking and state persistence
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this PyLabRobot protocol with tip tracking, volume tracking, saved deck layout, saved resource state, and clear recovery steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-instrument workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PyLabRobot workflow that combines liquid handling, temperature control, plate reading, and simulation-first validation. Include safety checks before hardware execution.

output_examples.input: User asks for a plate-to-plate transfer workflow.
output_examples.output: A structured protocol plan with required resources, backend choice, transfer sequence, tracking steps, and simulation checks.

output_examples.input: User asks whether a deck layout is ready for a Hamilton STAR run.
output_examples.output: A review that lists resource positions, missing placeholders, collision risks to verify, and required hardware validation.

output_examples.input: User asks how to connect an Opentrons OT-2 backend.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering robot IP address, HTTP API access, deck selection, simulation fallback, and pre-run safety review.

best_practices: Start with simulation and visualization before any physical hardware run.
Keep deck layouts, resource names, and saved states explicit and version controlled.
Review generated protocols with hardware manuals and local safety procedures before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated hardware code without simulation, dry-run review, and operator approval.
Do not reuse example IP addresses, file names, or deck positions without checking the actual lab setup.
Do not mix hardware backends in one protocol without confirming coordinate systems and device responsibilities.

faq.question: Can this skill control real laboratory hardware?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for PyLabRobot code that can control supported hardware when users run it in their own environment.

faq.question: Does it require physical instruments?
faq.answer: No. Many workflows can start with the Chatterbox simulation backend and visualizer.

faq.question: Which devices are covered?
faq.answer: The references cover liquid handlers, plate readers, heater shakers, incubators, centrifuges, pumps, resources, and visualization.

faq.question: Can it validate my lab safety process?
faq.answer: No. It can suggest review steps, but users must follow vendor manuals and institutional safety controls.

faq.question: Can it help migrate between robots?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains backend abstraction and highlights settings that must be verified for each robot.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples for real device control, local network connections, file writes, and environment-controlled hardware mode.
